summary: The q6 dataset context provides only aggregate issue counts, showing a uniform distribution of exactly 100 issues across all ten problems (q1-q10) with no qualitative proof-status detail. Because every problem carries an identical issue load and no per-issue content, traction, recency, or solvability signals are available, no problem can be distinguished as more active or closer to resolution. Meaningful prioritization requires richer metadata that the current context does not supply.
problems: No problem can be identified as having more activity or traction: all ten problems (q1-q10) report an identical 100 issues each, providing no differentiating signal.
The 'Proof Status' section lacks any actual status labels (e.g., open, in-progress, solved); it only repeats raw issue counts, making proof-progress assessment impossible.
Common patterns in open issues cannot be extracted because no issue-level descriptions, tags, or categories are present in the provided context.
Solvability-soon estimates are unsupported: there is no data on issue difficulty, dependencies, or partial-proof completeness for any problem.
The uniform 100-issue count is suspicious and may indicate a placeholder, truncation, or data-export cap rather than genuine measured activity.
highlights: Complete coverage: all ten problems in the q6 dataset have associated issue data, with none missing or empty.
Consistent data shape across q1-q10 suggests a stable, automated collection pipeline that can be enriched.
The dataset is well-scoped (exactly 10 problems), making it tractable for a focused weekly review once detail is added.

suggested_todos.title: Investigate why every problem reports exactly 100 issues — confirm whether this is a real count or a data cap/placeholder
Enrich the dataset export to include per-issue metadata (status, tags, last-updated, difficulty) to enable real prioritization
Add genuine proof-status labels (open/in-progress/blocked/solved) so the Proof Status section reflects progress rather than raw counts
Once enriched, run a follow-up analysis to rank problems by traction and identify near-solvable candidates
Define and document the issue-counting methodology to prevent ambiguous aggregate-only reports
